body.summary: Red‑team analysis of edit‑intent and collision registry

findings.description: Two lanes could write distinct symbols to the same file if they acquire leases on different logical blocks and bypass the central Registry by using low‑level API calls (fs.open with O_DIRECT). The Registry only tracks symbolic names, not raw byte offsets, allowing a race where Lane A writes symbol X at offset 0 while Lane B writes symbol Y at offset 4 without lease conflict.

If a lease expires but the process holding the lease crashes before releasing, another lane may acquire a new lease while the original process still has the file descriptor open, enabling concurrent writes.

Runtime files are stored under .runtime/<lane>/ which is not part of the public Registry. A malicious lane could write to a path like .runtime/shared/tmp that is later read by another lane via a relative include, bypassing lease checks.

A lane can rename a file after obtaining a lease on the original name, then a second lane obtains a lease on the new name, leading to a window where both lanes access the same inode under different names.
Operators with direct shell access can invoke `node scripts/lease-write.js` with `--force` flag, skipping the lease acquisition step entirely.

recommendations: Extend Registry to track inode and offset ranges
Enforce lease expiry detection with process‑liveness checks
Add AuditedPath validation for any .runtime read/write
Introduce a lease-aware wrapper for all file operations
Require all file writes to go through the Registry‑mediated edit‑intent API
Implement cryptographic signing of lease acquisitions
Add runtime monitoring for unexpected inode reuse
Quarantine lanes that bypass lease checks
